findings.title: D3's "publish-writer-events-db-write-degrade-open-medium" debt entry justifies an accepted silent-swallow with a JSON backstop that does not exist for the code paths that actually call it -- a swallowed events.db write is permanent data loss, not the claimed "temporary staleness window"
findings.description:
write_event/write_publish_result catch Exception, log, and return None -- a DB failure cannot break a publish run, per the module docstring.
The new debt_registry.toml entry (status=open, severity=medium) justifies this as safe because webui_app/helpers/history.py calls write_publish_result only after the primary publish-history.json write already succeeded, and events/reconcile.py::project_on_read independently re-derives events.db rows from that same JSON file on every health-dashboard load.
This claim does not hold for the current code: write_publish_result is called ONLY from webui_app/helpers/history.py (grep-confirmed, no other caller in src/ or webui_app/), and that file's own docstring states: "As of U2, events.db is the sole write target -- history_store is no longer written here" (Plan 2026-05-28-007 U2, predates this D3 sweep).
I traced every caller of the three _push_history_* helpers (webui_app/routes/pipeline_publish.py, webui_app/routes/batch.py, webui_app/scheduler.py::_publish_draft_job, webui_app/routes/checkpoint.py, webui_app/api/v1/pipeline.py) and none of them write publish-history.json before or after calling write_publish_result.
The only writer of that file, src/backlink_publisher/_util/history_write.py::append_published_rows, is dead code in production -- it is imported only by tests/integration/test_pipeline_e2e.py and tests/test_webui_api_v1_pipeline.py, never by src/ or webui_app/ runtime code.
Separately, for the majority of platforms (API-tier adapters routed through sdk/_publish_runtime.py::publish_inprocess -- blogger, notion, hackmd, hashnode, gitlab/github pages, linkedin, tumblr, wordpress.com, mataroa, rentry, substack, telegraph, writeas, zenn, config_driven-based platforms, etc.), there is also no checkpoint file (checkpoint-based projection only exists for the CLI-subprocess/browser-tier path per sdk/api.py's publish() docstring), so there is no backstop of any kind.
Net effect: a swallowed sqlite write failure (locked DB, disk full, corruption) for a WebUI-initiated, API-tier publish means the publish result (platform, live URL, timestamp, status) is not persisted anywhere -- not in events.db (write failed) and not in any JSON/checkpoint file (nothing writes one on this path) -- so it never surfaces on the History page, in equity-ledger, or to any dedup/ground-truth check that reads events.db.
This is a genuine, currently-unaddressed reliability gap whose registry classification (open/medium, reasoning framed around a bounded staleness window) understates the real risk; the entry's own safety argument is factually stale and should be corrected, or the site should be escalated to fix-now (e.g. write a durable fallback record, or surface the failure to the operator) rather than left open under an inaccurate rationale.
findings.recommendation:
Either (a) restore a genuine independent backstop for the WebUI/API-tier path -- e.g. have webui_app/helpers/history.py also append to publish-history.json (or another durable JSON/file-based store) before calling write_publish_result, so project_on_read has something real to re-derive from, or (b) surface write_event/write_publish_result failures to the operator (a visible "history write degraded" banner/log alert) instead of a bare log.warning, since the current failure mode is invisible until an operator notices a missing history row.
At minimum, correct the debt_registry.toml rationale to reflect that no publish-history.json write currently precedes this call, and re-evaluate the severity/status given the actual (lack of) backstop.

residual_risks.title: D2's medium_browser Save-Draft fix is correctly non-retryable and correctly handled by callers -- verified, not a gap
residual_risks.description:
The new ExternalServiceError raised on Save-Draft click failure (src/backlink_publisher/publishing/adapters/medium_browser.py, mode='draft' branch) is passed through retry_transient_call with is_retryable=lambda exc: isinstance(exc, PlaywrightTimeoutError); since ExternalServiceError is caught by retry_transient_call's own belt-and-suspenders "except (ExternalServiceError, DependencyError): raise" before is_retryable is ever consulted, this exception structurally cannot be retried, avoiding a duplicate-draft double-execution.
At the dispatch layer, src/backlink_publisher/publishing/reliability/transient_policy.py::classify_transient defaults to FAIL_FAST for any ExternalServiceError lacking a _blp_pre_create_429 stamp (which this exception does not have), so _registry_dispatch.dispatch() will not silently fall through to the next adapter in the medium chain and attempt a second draft/publish.
At the CLI layer, src/backlink_publisher/cli/publish_backlinks/_engine.py::_dispatch_adapter_publish already has an explicit "except ExternalServiceError" arm (record failure, continue to next row) so the new exception cannot hang or crash a publish run. No downstream code assumes Save Draft never raises for the specific status="drafted" return value -- other adapters' own "drafted" returns are unaffected, and the WebUI does not call MediumBrowserAdapter.publish() directly outside the dispatch/CLI path.

residual_risks.title: D3's second open/medium item (projector-run-safe-inline-flush) is more defensible than its sibling -- the claimed backstop appears genuine
residual_risks.description:
Unlike publish-writer-events-db-write-degrade, events/projector.py::project_run_safe's inline flush operates on a checkpoint JSON file that is written independently by the CLI publish/resume run itself (before project_run_safe is even invoked), and events/_project_helpers.py::cursor_save only commits inside the same per-source transaction as the reducer's data writes -- so a failed flush leaves no projection_cursor row, and events/reconcile.py::_collect_sources will pick the same checkpoint file up again as "stranded" on the next dashboard load.
This is a genuinely different, already-persisted source being retried, not a degenerate re-invocation of the same failing call. The registry's own caveat (surfacing only happens on the next dashboard load, an unmeasured latency window) is a reasonable, honestly-scoped reason to leave it "open" rather than "accepted" -- reviewed and found consistent with the code, in contrast to the mis-justified sibling entry flagged above.

testing_gaps.title: No test pins the no-retry guarantee for the new medium_browser Save-Draft ExternalServiceError
testing_gaps.description:
tests/test_adapter_medium_browser.py::test_save_draft_click_failure_raises_instead_of_reporting_drafted asserts the exception type/message but does not assert that sync_playwright (or the browser/context) was only entered once -- i.e. it does not directly pin "this failure is never retried." The no-retry behavior is currently guaranteed structurally by retry_transient_call's hard-coded ExternalServiceError/DependencyError bare-raise (not by this adapter's own is_retryable predicate), so today's guarantee holds, but a future refactor that changes is_retryable's contract or moves this raise site would not be caught by this test.
Consider adding an assertion on mock_sync_pw's call count (or equivalent) to make the non-retry property explicit.
